speaker: interviewer
text: --of Max Eisen. Could you please continue with your story, Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I-- we leave town, I'd like to come back to our neighbor, who was a-- [CLEARS THROAT] a very good friend of ours, [CLEARS THROAT] a Christian lady. [COUGHS] Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since we were orthodox, and as long as we had this nanny living with us, you know, so in the winter, she was starting the fireplaces so that we wouldn't freeze, you know? Because if you're an Orthodox Jew, you must not light a fire. You know, the law is that you cannot do any work. So she was lighting the fires, you know, in our quarters, my grandfather, and my uncle and aunt. And when she had to leave in 1939, well, this friend of ours, she was doing this for us, you know? And otherwise, I don't know what we could have done. I just can't figure, you know, how we would have lived, you know? Because there was no such thing as central heating, you see? Every home was heated by wood stoves and such. So she was a very great help to us. The closest Gentile friend--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that the family had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. She was a-- I can't think of any others, offhand, that-- there was nobody that was that close to us, that she-- you know. And so here we are, we're going back to the schoolhouse where we are being taken out of the schoolhouse this one morning. All of the Jewish people from this town, approximately 300 of us, altogether, men, women, children, and we're marched through town, guarded by gendarmes and taking-- we are taken down to the train station, and we're loaded up on a train. This time, it was just a regular train. And we are taken to a neighboring city, the capital city of our province, which is called Kassa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, on the march through town, how did the other townspeople behave? Were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some people who were yelling at us, some derogatory remarks, you know? You know, stinking Jews, you know, you're going to get what's coming to you, things like that. I remember being very ashamed that I was marching in this, you know? And people were yelling these terrible things at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a very-- I felt really terrible, you know? I said, this is my town. And all of a sudden, where I was born, and I lived here for 14 years, and I could sense that I am different than they are, you know? They are standing on-- on a firm ground. They are at home, and I don't know where I am going, you know? I felt really bad, you know, and very uncertain of what's going to happen to me, you know? And I could-- so these were the feelings that I had. And we were not treated very nicely, of course. So then, we arrived at the train station, and we were put on the train. And as I said, we came into Kassa, and we were marched down to the-- to this big synagogue yard where they-- the Jewish community was looking after all these people brought in from all over the province.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kassa was the provincial city of the province. And the system in Hungary was that they were collecting all these Jews into central places in the capital cities of the provinces. The plans were, you know-- which we didn't know-- but these were the plans to bring us to near major railroad yards where they can put us on trains and ship us straightforward to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told us, in the meantime, to, if we have any relatives, to go and move in with them. So we did have some relatives. Their name was Davidowicz. And [CLEARS THROAT] these were cousins. And we moved in with them, our whole family. And I remember, we were sleeping on the floors there, because they had an apartment. And they-- it was just an apartment, sufficient for them, for themselves. And all of a sudden, we all moved in. How many of us? Let's see, 2, 4, 6, 11, 12 people moved in into this apartment, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we stayed with them for approximately 10 days to 2 weeks. And I was going out into-- into-- into the town with some of my other friends from-- they lived in the neighboring area. I knew the city Kassa, because I was working there as an apprentice. And this was the first time when we were going through the streets we saw a troop of SS soldiers marching by, you know, and with their jackboots and their hobnails. And that was a frightening sight. And gradually, the laws came. And they announced this. And posters were put up on the walls. And they said, these streets, from here to here, they have to move into the brickyard by a certain date. So they were sort of tightening the whole area.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not in a ghetto. You know, we were living with our relatives. And many others lived with some other people. But they were cutting down the area, you know, so they announced that this section has to move out by a certain area, at the pain of whatever, you know? And-- and they have to be in the brickyard. And so we had to move. One day, we had to walk up to this brickyard from where we were living. It was quite a march, you know? We carried-- we carried our bundles, and we came into this big brickyard. What did you have in your bundle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had some clothing, and we had some food that we bought again, you know, in Kassa, you know? And so, we took some of this food with us. We didn't have much, you know? We only had a little bundle, you know? [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came into this brickyard. And it was-- this was a huge place. We were put into a big shed. I think there must have been close to 2,000 people in this big shed. And I know, from statistics that I read post-war, that there were 18,000 people collected in this brickyard. And you can multiply this by all the other provinces, you know, where they were collecting the Jews. So you can see 18,000 people living in a area like this. They were living somewhere, put into these big kilns where they were burning the bricks. All I remember is red dust all over the place. And it was terrible, you know? There was no running water. I remember the first time they were building latrines here. And it was a terrible sight, you know? I mean, that's the first time I saw anything like this. You know, the smell was awful, and there was no privacy. Children were screaming. And there was a kitchen put up. And again, it comes down to that bowl of soup, you know? This bowl of soup becomes a real big thing, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the brickyard was fenced around. And I remember gendarmes were guarding the brickyard, all around. And the Nazis started this brainwashing, because, almost instantly, and perhaps a few days after we arrived there, this SS officer came into the brickyard, and he made a speech. And he was telling us in Hungarian and German that we're going to be resettled in the east.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, he said this every day. And he said that families will be together. And you can take everything you have with you. You know, we didn't have much, but you know, when you have that little, you know, it becomes all of a sudden a big possession, you know? I realize that now. And you're sort of holding onto something. It-- it-- it ties you to your home, and it gives you some security. So but when they said, your families will be together, and your work-- you'll be working together, that really sounded good, you know? And we sort of couldn't wait to come to this promised land, you know, wherever that was, you know? We had no idea. We thought it was Eastern Hungary. We never thought it was going to be in Poland, you know? So this went on for about a good 2 weeks, this brainwashing. And eventually, finally, the transports started to leave. And I think there were three transports that left from this brickyard in Kassa. And when the transports were leaving, they were asking for-- for kids to come and clean up the mess, you know? People leave a lot of trash and stuff behind, and we had to clean up everything to, sort of like, to wipe out all the evidence, you know, that was left behind, you know? And you know, we found some coins and things that people dropped from their pockets, and stuff like that. So it was kind of a fun thing, you know? You never knew what you were going to come up with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, the third transport arrived. And we were on this third transport. And we were taken by the gendarmes to these cattle cars that were lined up there, just 10 minutes away from our place. And we marched up there, and we were loaded up in these cattle cars. And we were crammed in tight. Must have been 100 people in this boxcar. And initially, there was a pail of water. And there was another pail there for sanitary purposes. And all of a sudden, we saw a troop of SS soldiers come up. And the Hungarian went over to these Nazis there, and he was talking to them. And the Hungarian saluted off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these SS soldiers, they came to every boxcar, and they just grabbed the doors. And the doors were shut. And these hooks were locked, you know? And that was a real shock, you know? All of a sudden, we realized, you know, this is not so good, you know? And so, here we are on this train. And-- How did the people react at-- at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, it was a kind of a thing, you know, that-- you know, the grandparents and the parents, they didn't want to upset the children, you know, this type of thing. When I think back, you know, everybody was trying to sort of keep a good face. You know what I mean? It's like, you know-- and there are women, and grandmothers, and you know-- so everybody was trying to be very civil about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and, you know, people are saying, don't push, you know? There's no room. You couldn't sit down, you know? There was no room to sit down. So it was getting very hot. And this little bit of water-- you know, whoever was near the pail of water, drank the water. And the others couldn't get any water. You couldn't walk to this pail, because you were stuck in a corner. You couldn't get through, you know? You were sort of standing locked in, you know? And [CLEARS THROAT] so naturally, that water was gone in no time. And this pail, you know, was full, you know? And it started to smell. And I couldn't even get to this pail, you know? So this was a-- you know, I was on this train for approximately 3 and 1/2 days. The train stopped at many stations. And we were screaming, you know? There was a little opening on this cattle car, you know, with some bars. And we were yelling to give us water, you know? But the doors were never opened. And there was never any water given to us, you know? So [CLEARS THROAT] some people died. I remember, it is the first time I saw a dead body that-- well, that was the second time, actually. I forgot to tell you about my aunt that passed away just before. But anyway, some people died. And many others, they sort of lost their minds, you know? They started-- they were yelling and screaming. They were claustrophobic, you know? And this was absolutely a horrible experience, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived. The train stopped after 3 and 1/2 days, approximately, in the middle of the night. And all of sudden, we heard [CLEARS THROAT] lots of yelling. And I heard dogs barking. And we heard 'Raus' in German. And we didn't know where we were. But finally, we could hear doors were opening up. And our door was opened up too. And there was a-- a man standing there in a striped outfit with a little cap on this head, striped cap. And he had a walking stick in his hand. And he started to yell at us, 'Raus'-- 'Out,' in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, after standing 3, 4 days in one position, you know, you can hardly-- you can't even move, you know? Your feet are all swollen, you know? And you are completely disoriented. And when you start, your brain starts to engage, you're starting to look for your little bundles. Where is everything, you know? And they're yelling at you. And they say, no, don't worry about your bundle, you know? You'll get it the next day. And they were hooking these walking sticks around people's legs or necks. And they yanked you right out of this boxcar, you know? And people were falling down on this platform.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember now how we got my grandparents down from this boxcar. But there was my-- we were on this platform. My mother held the baby. And my two younger brothers, my grandmother and my aunt on each side of him, and my grandfather, and my father and uncle, we're all marching. And all of a sudden, they separated us. They separated the men from the women. The old men went with the women. And we were just split in a matter of seconds. And I was left here on-- with my father and uncle. We were being marched forward. And there were some doctors at the end of the platform. I remember his white gloves. And they were looking at us. And my family, my grandmother, my mother, and my two younger brothers, they just disappeared from our sight. And my father and uncle were sent to the right side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And came my turn, and I was standing in front of this SS doctor in his black outfit, black uniform, with high boots, and his white gloves. And he looks at me, and he says to me, how old are you? I said, I'm 19. I don't know why I said that. And I sort of stretched myself out as far as I could. And he looks at me, and he says, go that way. And I went with my father and my uncle to the right side. And we didn't know what this all meant, but we figured that we'll see the rest of our family soon, you know? It was only a matter-- this is what we were told, it's just a matter of, you know, the next day you'll see them. And it was handled, you know, very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called this processing at transport. And I know now that it-- they allowed one hour to process a transport. This was 1944. And Hungarian transports were coming practically around the clock. And they had to process one transport, so the other one was coming behind. And we were taken into a barrack. There were soldiers, SS soldiers, with these fellows in their outfits with bags. And they said to empty our pockets. We had to take everything out, documents, whatever money we had, watches, drop everything in, into these bags. Anything that we had of value had to be dropped in. And the next thing was we had to get undressed, fold up our clothes. And the only thing we were allowed to keep were our boots. No underwear, no socks, just your boots. And the next stage was we went into this barrack. And there were stools there. And there were a whole bunch of stools. And there were fellows with shears already cutting a hair-- your hair. And so, they cut our hair down under our arm and our crotch. And the next stage was they took us into these showers. And we must have been, I don't know, 100 people in this shower room. And they told us to put our boots down near the wall and to get under the shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the shower was opened, and it was hot water. I remember this SS guy on the controls, on these valves. He was opening up boiling hot water, and then cold water. And they were having a real fun-- you know, I said, this is-- what is this place, you know? And all of a sudden, we saw these guys in these striped suits. They came running in, and they were grabbing our boots. And they were running away with our boots. So my father ran and grabbed all our boots, you know? And he says, get your boots and put it under your arms. And so I was standing under the water, you know, under the shower with these boots under my arm. Because he realized that, if the boots are gone, you know, we are finished, you know? Without boots, you know, you-- you got these wooden clogs. So you know, when you came to this place, everything-- we came with good boots. And these guys that were there, they were looking for these things. So we came out of the shower naked, and we were taken into a barrack. We were put into these-- into these bunks, triple bunks. And there were just wooden planks there. There were five of us on a bunk, naked. And morning arrived very-- just a little while after. And we heard a gong, you know? There was a lager gong, you know, that they-- it was like a big bell. And this meant that you had to get out of your bunk. And we were lined up and counted. And they came with a metal container. There was some liquid on it. It was called morning tea. It looked like tea, but it was-- it didn't taste like tea. And we-- we received a metal dish-- it was called a schissel in German-- that we were drinking this tea from. You had to line up. And the one guy was ladling it in into these schissels. And you know, we-- we were drinking it, because we had no spoons, you know? There was no spoons or forks. You just had a schissel, and that's the way we ate. You always drank from this pot, you see? So when these guys brought the tea, we asked them, what happened to our families? So they said, well-- at that time, we looked around, and we saw in the little distance away, this huge building that had flames and smoke coming out. We didn't know what this building was. We could smell something. There was a very bad smell. It was like burning-- I remember, you know, from the blacksmith at home, when he was shoeing horses. And you know, when they put the hot shoe on the horse's hooves, it was smelling like-- you know, like burning flesh. And that was the smell that was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, you see, they went up in smoke. And at first, you know, we couldn't comprehend what he was talking about. And he said, that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I looked around a little while later. There was a rail going on just outside our fence where we were in this-- in these barracks. And there were two prisoners pushing a little, like, a mining cart on these rails. And bodies were-- you know, legs were hanging out. And you know-- and I could never understand, you know, how could people have such thin legs, you know, just skin and bones, you know? These were naked bodies, you know, in this cart, you know? This was a horrible thing. So you know, we realized where we were. And we-- we knew that we will never see the rest of our family. So we were organized into different work groups. So they asked for farmers. So my father said, well, we're all farmers, you know? We-- you know, that would be not a bad place to work, because we will be, probably, working around some potatoes, or something on the field, you know? So we said, we're farmers. And we were organized in this group, about 200 of us. Prior to that, we were given our rags, you know, these striped outfits. And we were given our tattooed numbers. And my number is 89,892. And my father's was 93, and my uncle's was 94. What was the tattooing process like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tattooing process was-- there were these guys, and they had a sort of something like a pen and with a needle on it. And you had to go, and there was somebody standing with a-- with a board, you know? And the numbers were all recorded, you see? Because you are only known by your number.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not known by a name, once you were there. Once you got a number, you were a number. And this is the way you traveled from one camp to another, as a number, you see? Your name was no longer-- you were no longer a human being, you see? So [CLEARS THROAT] this is the way the-- I have a very small number. And some people had big numbers, you know? It depends, you know, who was doing the tattooing, you see? So but we sort of felt that, you know, once we had a tattooed number, we thought, that, well, we are going to be working here, you know? Because it sort of started to sink in that, you know, some people were burned right away. And we didn't-- still didn't know the process that was going on there. But we figured, if they went to all this trouble of giving us numbers, at least we can work, you know? I mean, we didn't know right away just what the process was going to be here, you see? So after all this, we were lined up. And this was Birkenau. We were marched over to Auschwitz, which was about 6 kilometers away from here. [CLEARS THROAT] And we had to march in A military fashion. They were yelling at us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were led by SS soldiers and a kapo. A kapo was usually called A person who was the head of a working group. He was a-- he was the boss. That's what a kapo was. But that's the way they were called, kapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they usually carried a heavy rubber truncheon. And these kapos, they were in charge of us. And they could do practically anything with us, you know? They could kill you on the spot. And they did not have to apologize to anybody for this, because the Germans, the Nazis, have them-- have given them this power, you know? Because it was these people that ran the infrastructure in the camp, you see, and the work details. So we were marched into Auschwitz. And I remember coming to Auschwitz. I see it's a huge place, you know, with-- with very high electrified, double electrified wire fence all around. And I see these towers all around, with machine guns and searchlights. And there's a big guard tower right at the gate. And there's a big gate that says 'Arbeit macht frei.' And I could hear music, you know? This was just around lunch time, when we were marching in. And I said, what's going on here? You know, I couldn't figure out what this is all about. There's music here. And you know, I didn't know-- I looked at these brick buildings, and they looked not bad. It was better than the barracks in Birkenau, because those barracks in Birkenau were wooden things. They looked like stables, you know? I said, this is not bad-looking place, you know? My first impression, you know? There was music, and these big buildings, and lots of SS men bristling around the gate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the gate was opened, and we were marched in. We were marched to a barrack. And-- [CLEARS THROAT] and this kapo said, well, you are going into room up on the second floor. I think these barracks had three floors. And they were divided into so many rooms. I can't tell you exactly how many. So we each had a bunk this time. They were triple bunks, and I wound up on the top bunk, right on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were in a group. It was called a [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] sort of means farming type of a group. And we thought that that's what we are going to do. [CLEARS THROAT] So the kapo told us that-- he gave us the-- this initiation speech, you know, that he says, whatever I say, you know, this goes, you know, because there's no way out of this place. Here, you'll die of hunger, or sickness, or beating, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [CLEARS THROAT] he was talking in German. Some were talking in Polish. And you know, it was very confusing for me. And I was very lucky that my father and uncle were there, because they spoke German, and they understood some Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized right away that, if you didn't understand what orders were given to you, you were a martyr right away. I mean, you know, you-- you would have been beaten to death instantly. So they were sort of giving me the clues what has to be done, you see? So this was our initiation. And we-- we received our meal. It-- they brought us soup. And I remember this first experience with this soup that they brought down. I tell you, the-- I don't think I'll ever forget the terrible smell of this soup. It was absolutely revolting, you know? And I was so hungry, you know, because I was, what? About 3, 4 days on this transport. And all I had was a cup of tea that morning. I had nothing to eat in those 4 days, practically. And they brought this soup, and I just couldn't put it-- you know, I-- I wouldn't touch it. And my father and uncle, they practically forced it down my throat, you know? And you know, had they not been there, I wouldn't have, probably-- [CHUCKLES] I would have not eaten it, and I would have been a goner fast. But, you know, they forced this soup down. And eventually, the time came when the soup tasted pretty good, you know? But this first experience was absolutely terrible. So this was my first day in Auschwitz. After this lunch, they allowed us to walk around in camp. And I could see the tremendous size of this camp, you know? It was absolutely immense. And I could see many prisoners, you know? I call myself a prisoner. I really hate to say this, but I feel that I have committed a crime. But we were called haftlings, you know? And so, I saw many people that were going around in some pretty good-looking outfits, you know? And these were the people that ran the-- the infrastructure of the camp. They were called a prominent people. And these are mostly Polish people and criminals, German criminals, who were taken out of jails in Germany. And they ran the infrastructure. And Polish political prisoners, they were there since 1942. Because actually, Auschwitz was built for Polish prisoners. So those that were still alive, they got themselves into the top positions. They were the [GERMAN]. It was called a lagteraltester, which was a prisoner, and the blockaltesters, and the [? room elders. ?] You see, every block, every barrack, had a block leader. And each room had a room leader. So this was the hierarchy in Auschwitz, you see? And these people, they already had a infrastructure built for themselves, supported by the Germans. Because you have to understand, you know, which I understood later, that there was tremendous amount of money that-- and jewelry that people were able to get ahold of from the transports that came into Auschwitz. And because some prisoners-- there was a group called Canada. And it was an odd name, you know? Why they called it Canada, because they thought that Canada is a country with such tremendous wealth. And this group, Canada, was the one that had to empty all the transport wagons after the people were taken out. They had to take out everything, and take it to a place, and search out every piece, because in bread, you know, people used to hide some money or something. And they had to open up every tube of toothpaste to see whether people were not hiding some diamonds or stuff like that in it, you know? So-- finished?
